name: abstract
plan:
Paragraph 1: Introduce the background of Extremely Large-Scale MIMO (XL-MIMO) in 6G and the resulting near-field spatial non-stationarity, specifically the Visibility Region (VR) phenomenon where users are only covered by a subset of antennas. 
Paragraph 2: Highlight the limitations of traditional full-array processing (e.g., severe noise accumulation, prohibitive computational complexity) and fully-connected AI/GNN methods (scalability bottlenecks). 
Paragraph 3: Propose 'Graph-VR-Det', a novel graph-based near-field detection scheme. Explain its core mechanisms: pilot-energy-based dynamic VR discovery to construct sparse bipartite graphs, and a Sparse Message Passing Neural Network (Sparse-GNN) that restricts evidence processing to valid edges. Mention the frequency-aware extension to compensate for wideband beam splitting at THz frequencies. 
Paragraph 4: Summarize the key results. State that the proposed method achieves up to a 3.3x inference speedup over fully-connected MPNNs, exhibits a unique graph regularization effect that surpasses dense networks at optimal sparsity, and demonstrates strong zero-shot scalability to 1024-antenna arrays without retraining.

name: introduction
plan:
Paragraph 1: Discuss the evolution toward 6G communications, emphasizing the adoption of XL-MIMO and sub-THz/THz bands to achieve ultra-high data rates. Explain the paradigm shift from far-field planar waves to near-field spherical waves. 
Paragraph 2: Detail the spatial non-stationarity in near-field XL-MIMO. Define the Visibility Region (VR) concept and explain how traditional linear detectors (ZF/MMSE) suffer from massive matrix inversion complexity and noise enhancement when processing the entire array. 
Paragraph 3: Review the rise of Deep Learning and Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) for MIMO detection. Point out the critical gap: existing GNNs rely on fully-connected graph topologies, which scale poorly (O(NK)) and suffer from the 'curse of dimensionality' and memory overflow (OOM) in XL-MIMO scenarios. 
Paragraph 4: Outline the specific contributions of this paper: 1) A dynamic, pilot-based VR discovery algorithm that extracts sparse graph topologies without prior CSI; 2) A Sparse-GNN detector that reduces complexity from linear to the total antennas to linear to the VR size; 3) A frequency-aware feature mechanism to combat wideband beam splitting; 4) Extensive evaluations showing a sparsity-induced regularization effect and zero-shot generalization to 1024 antennas.

name: system model
plan:
Paragraph 1: Narrowband Near-Field XL-MIMO System. Define the physical geometry of the Uniform Linear Array (ULA) with N antennas and K single-antenna users randomly distributed in the Fresnel near-field region. Formulate the spherical wave channel model based on exact distances. 
Paragraph 2: Spatial Non-Stationarity and VR Mask. Mathematically define the VR mask matrix M, where each user is only visible to a contiguous subset of antennas (defined by a radius R_v). Explain how this creates a naturally sparse channel matrix. 
Paragraph 3: Wideband OFDM System and Beam Splitting. Extend the model to a wideband OFDM architecture (e.g., 0.1 THz center frequency, 10 GHz bandwidth, F subcarriers). Formulate the frequency-dependent phase shifts and path loss, explicitly describing how this causes the near-field beam splitting effect. 
Paragraph 4: Signal Transmission and Problem Formulation. Define the received signal model y = Hx + n. Briefly formulate the traditional detection problem and introduce the Genie-Aided VR-ZF and VR-MMSE as theoretical performance upper bounds.

name: Proposed Method
plan:
Paragraph 1: Overall Architecture of Graph-VR-Det. Formulate the XL-MIMO detection task as a node classification/regression problem on a bipartite graph consisting of antenna nodes and user nodes. 
Paragraph 2: Dynamic Visibility Region Discovery. Detail the pilot transmission phase. Explain how the receiver calculates the energy of the received pilot signals to dynamically select the Top-S antennas for each user, generating a sparse binary adjacency matrix (edge_index) without requiring perfect CSI. 
Paragraph 3: Sparse Message Passing Neural Network (Sparse-GNN). Describe the node and edge feature initialization (embedding layer). Detail the sparse message passing mechanism utilizing scatter-add operations, restricting the Antenna-to-User (A2U) and User-to-Antenna (U2A) updates strictly to the discovered VR edges. 
Paragraph 4: Frequency-Aware Wideband Extension. Explain the joint frequency-domain graph mapping (averaging pilot energy across subcarriers for a stable topology). Introduce the frequency-aware edge features, where normalized frequency offsets are concatenated with channel coefficients to help the GNN learn and compensate for beam splitting. 
Paragraph 5: Complexity Analysis. Provide a theoretical comparison of computational complexity (FLOPs) and memory footprint among Full-MMSE, Full-MPNN, and the proposed Sparse-GNN, highlighting the reduction from O(N^3) or O(NK) to O(SK).

name: Numerical Results
plan:
Paragraph 1: Simulation Setup. Detail the dataset generation parameters (N=256, K=8, F=16, 0.1 THz, SNR ranges) and the training hyperparameters (epochs, learning rate, hidden dimensions) for both narrowband and wideband scenarios. 
Paragraph 2: VR Discovery and the Sparsity Trade-off. Analyze the VR Hit Rate across different sparsity levels (S=38, 77, 128) and SNRs. Discuss the performance-complexity trade-off, highlighting the surprising 'Regularization Effect' where an optimal sparsity (S=180) filters out noise/interference and actually outperforms the fully-connected MPNN baseline. 
Paragraph 3: Model Capacity and Inference Efficiency. Compare the BER and inference time of Full-MPNN vs. Sparse-GNN under varying network capacities (hidden_dim 64/128, layers 4/6). Emphasize the consistent ~3x speedup achieved by Sparse-GNN. 
Paragraph 4: Wideband Performance and Beam Splitting. Evaluate the Freq-Aware Sparse-GNN against Genie-Aided VR-ZF/MMSE in the wideband scenario. Acknowledge the error floor at high SNRs (10^-3) but validate its practical effectiveness given it operates without perfect VR masks. 
Paragraph 5: Zero-Shot Scale-Up to Massive Arrays. Present the zero-shot generalization results where the model trained on N=256 is directly tested on N=1024. Highlight the massive drop in BER due to array gain and the extreme computational advantage (avoiding OOM, ~8.2ms inference time).
figure: Mandate 3 pgfplots: 1. A line chart showing Narrowband BER vs SNR, comparing the Full-MPNN baseline against Sparse-GNN at different sparsity levels (S=77, 128, 180) to visually demonstrate the regularization effect where S=180 outperforms the baseline. 2. A line chart showing Wideband BER vs SNR, comparing the Freq-Aware Sparse-GNN (S=128) against Genie-Aided VR-ZF and VR-MMSE, clearly showing the performance tracking and the high-SNR error floor. 3. A grouped bar chart comparing Inference Time (Speedup) between Full-MPNN and Sparse-GNN across four different model capacities (Config 1 to 4), demonstrating the consistent ~3x computational reduction.

name: Conclusion
plan:
Paragraph 1: Restate the primary challenge addressed in this paper: the prohibitive complexity and noise accumulation of signal detection in near-field XL-MIMO systems. 
Paragraph 2: Summarize the proposed Graph-VR-Det framework, emphasizing its two core innovations: pilot-driven dynamic VR discovery and the frequency-aware sparse message passing architecture. 
Paragraph 3: Highlight the most significant quantitative findings: the ~3x inference speedup, the discovery of the graph sparsity regularization effect that improves BER over dense networks, and the robust zero-shot scalability to 1024 antennas. 
Paragraph 4: Suggest directions for future research, such as mitigating the high-SNR error floor in wideband scenarios, extending the framework to multi-antenna users, or exploring hardware-efficient implementations of sparse scatter operations.
